components.name: a11yBadge
components.description: Accessibility contrast badge — shown inline on color tokens.

props.name: variant
props.description: Contrast compliance level. Use 'pass' for basic compliance, 'aa' for WCAG AA, 'aaLarge' for WCAG AA with larger text, 'fail' for non-compliant pairs, and 'default' for neutral state.

props.name: disabled
props.description: Visually disables the badge when contrast data is unavailable or pending.

props.name: children
props.description: Badge label text such as 'AA', 'AAA', 'Fail', or a contrast ratio value like '4.5:1'.

props.name: onClick
props.description: Optional callback to handle badge interaction, such as opening a detailed contrast report or color adjustment panel.

components.usage: Use a11yBadge to display WCAG contrast compliance status inline on color tokens in design tools and documentation. Display the badge alongside color swatches to communicate at a glance whether a color combination meets AA, AA-Large, or fails accessibility standards. This component is essential for guiding designers toward accessible color choices during the design process.
components.never: Never use this badge as a substitute for actual contrast testing—it should reflect real measured contrast ratios, not assumptions. Never display multiple contrast badges for the same color pairing, as this creates visual noise and contradicts the purpose of quick status communication. Never apply this badge to non-color contexts or as a generic status indicator outside of accessibility compliance.
components.compose: Render the a11yBadge as an inline element positioned adjacent to a color token preview or swatch. Pair it with the actual foreground and background colors being tested so the badge itself demonstrates the contrast it claims to measure.

aria.keyboard: Badge is not keyboard-interactive by default; use onClick only if the badge triggers an action like opening a modal.
aria.screenReader: Announce the contrast level and ratio, e.g., 'WCAG AA compliant, 4.5 to 1 contrast ratio'. Include the specific standard met (AA, AA-Large) or failure state so users understand compliance impact.

components.name: badge

props.name: variant
props.description: Visual style and semantic intent communicated by the badge color and styling

props.name: children
props.description: The text or content displayed inside the badge

props.name: disabled
props.description: Dims or mutes the badge to indicate inactive or archived status

props.name: onClick
props.description: Optional click handler; only use if badge needs to serve as a filter or interactive element in specific contexts

components.usage: Use badges to communicate status, category, or priority information in a compact, scannable format. Badges work best for labeling items in lists, tables, or inline with content where visual status needs to be quickly recognized without disrupting reading flow.
components.never: Never use badges as interactive elements—they are for display only and should not trigger actions on click. Never stack multiple badges without clear separation or nesting, as this creates visual clutter and makes individual statuses hard to distinguish. Never use badges to replace larger, more prominent alerts or notifications that require immediate user action.
components.compose: Badges integrate well alongside text labels or card titles to indicate state at a glance. Pair them with consistent spacing and ensure they sit inline or in a dedicated label area rather than floating loosely in layouts.

aria.keyboard: Badges are not keyboard interactive by default. If onClick is provided, ensure the parent element or wrapper handles keyboard focus and activation.
aria.screenReader: Screen readers announce the badge text and variant intent. Consider wrapping with aria-label if the badge alone does not provide sufficient context (e.g., 'Status: Active' instead of just 'Active').

components.name: Button
components.description: Primary, secondary, ghost, and danger button variants

default.staticClasses: inline-flex items-center justify-center text-sm font-medium transition-colors focus:outline-none
inline-flex items-center justify-center text-sm font-medium transition-colors focus:outline-none
inline-flex items-center justify-center text-sm font-medium transition-colors focus:outline-none
inline-flex items-center justify-center text-sm font-medium transition-colors focus:outline-none

components.usage: Use for primary actions where clear visual prominence is needed. One primary button per section maximum. Use secondary or ghost for supporting actions.
components.never: Never use more than one primary button per visual section. Never use disabled state without surfacing the reason to the user. Never use for navigation — use a link instead.
components.compose: Wrap in a ButtonGroup component when placing multiple buttons in a row. Use FormField wrapper when inside a form.

aria.keyboard: Enter or Space to activate. Tab to focus. Not focusable when disabled.
aria.screenReader: Announces label. Announces disabled state. Announces loading state as aria-busy.

components.name: card

props.name: variant
props.description: Visual style variant. 'default' includes standard card styling; 'body' provides minimal styling for flexible content composition.

props.name: children
props.description: Card content including header, body text, and interactive elements.

props.name: onClick
props.description: Callback fired when the card is clicked. When provided, ensures proper keyboard handling and ARIA attributes are applied.

props.name: disabled
props.description: When true, disables click interactions and applies disabled styling.

components.usage: Use cards to group related content and create visual hierarchy within layouts. Cards are ideal for presenting self-contained information units like articles, products, or settings panels that benefit from visual separation and shadow elevation.
components.never: Never use a card as a clickable button without proper ARIA attributes and keyboard support. Never nest cards within cards as this creates confusing visual and semantic hierarchy. Never rely solely on color or shadow for contrast—ensure text meets WCAG 1.4.3 contrast ratios independently.
components.compose: Cards work best as direct children in grid or list layouts. Pair cards with consistent spacing utilities and ensure header and body content are semantically structured with appropriate heading levels.

aria.role: If onClick is provided: button or link depending on navigation context. Otherwise: article or region depending on content semantics.
aria.keyboard: If interactive, card must be keyboard accessible via Tab key and activate with Enter or Space. Focus state must be visible with sufficient contrast.
aria.screenReader: Screen readers announce card role and any heading content within. If clickable, announce as button with descriptive label derived from card header or primary content.

components.name: codeBlock
components.description: Code display blocks — token output, JSON, production code.

props.name: variant
props.description: Visual style variant for the code block

props.name: children
props.description: The code content to display as plain text or pre-formatted string

props.name: language
props.description: Programming language for syntax highlighting and semantics

props.name: disabled
props.description: Disables interactive features like copying or selection

components.usage: Use codeBlock to display code snippets, JSON responses, token outputs, or production code that users need to read or reference. This component is ideal for documentation, API responses, configuration examples, and code samples that require syntax clarity and readability.
components.never: Never use codeBlock for editable code input—use a code editor component instead. Never rely solely on color to distinguish syntax highlighting; ensure sufficient contrast ratios meet WCAG 1.4.3. Never nest interactive elements inside codeBlock without clear visual separation from the code content.
components.compose: Wrap codeBlock with a container or card component when displaying multiple code samples together. Pair with a copy button or toolbar component positioned adjacent to the code block for user interactions like copying to clipboard.

aria.keyboard: Code blocks are not keyboard interactive by default; interactive toolbar buttons (if present) follow standard button keyboard patterns
aria.screenReader: Screen readers announce the code block as a region and read the code content sequentially; use aria-label to identify the code type or purpose

components.name: emptyState
components.description: Empty state — used when a list or section has no content.

default.staticClasses: flex items-center gap-3 rounded-lg border px-4 py-3 text-sm font-medium shadow-lg
flex items-center gap-3 rounded-lg border px-4 py-3 text-sm font-medium shadow-lg

components.name: tokenCard
components.description: Token editor card — the primary interactive element in the token list.
